title: West Virginia
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In West Virginia, indeterminate sentences carry minimum and maximum terms that are specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. There is no standard ratio between minimum and maximum terms. “Definite sentences” include terms to parole-release eligibility that are set by statutory formula at 25 percent.²

title: West Virginia
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 31 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 39 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 30 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2011 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 30 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 30 percent from 2020 to 2023.

text:

90 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.

title: West Virginia
text:
Offense Types
40 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 60 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people in prison past parole eligibility were property offenses (35 percent).

title: West Virginia
text:
Sentence Lengths
53 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.

title: West Virginia
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: West Virginia
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (50 percent). All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ Some years had to be excluded for this reason for West Virginia. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for West Virginia.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for West Virginia.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

¹ Alexis Lee Watts, Brendan Delaney, and Edward E. Rhine, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – West Virginia (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2019), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/west_virginia_parole_profile.pdf, 10.² Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: West Virginia (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, January 2022), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-07/west_virginia_doi_report_7_10_22.pdf, 6-9.³ WV Division of Corrections & Rehabilitation, FY 2023 Annual Report (Charleston, WV: West Virginia Division of Co(Charleston, WV: West Virginia Division of Corrections & Rehabilitation, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://dcr.wv.gov/Documents/FY23%20ANNUAL%20REPORT%20WVDCR.pdf, 33.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Lukac, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 6-9.⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: West Virginia

Prison Release Trends
From 2010 to 2020, the number of people released from prison increased 26 percent, from 2,659 in 2010 to 3,342 in 2020.





In the most recent year of data available, 70 percent of parole-eligible people released in West Virginia were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 1296 percent from 2010.

text:
Conditional Releases
Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. 66 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

title: West Virginia
text:
Measuring Disparities
Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:
(1) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group, (2) The average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility, including by offense type, and (3) The average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

Disparities Among People Incarcerated Past Parole Eligibility

Black, non-Hispanic people were 20 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility compared to White people, when accounting for population sizes in West Virginia. 

Hispanic people were 30 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility compared to White people, when accounting for population sizes in West Virginia.Analysis of disparities for Hispanic people should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.

Men were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.4 times higher than women, when accounting for prison population sizes in West Virginia.

title: West Virginia
section: Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility
text:
Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility

Race and EthnicitySex


The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. Black people and Hispanic people spent less time in prison after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Black people spent on average 6 months less past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 1 year and 7 months less past parole eligibility compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among drug offenses, where Black people spent on average 9 months more in prison compared to White people.






The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by sex for individuals still incarcerated. Women who were still incarcerated spent on average 1 year and 3 months less past parole eligibility compared to men.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and sex for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where men spent on average 2 years and 6 months more in prison compared to women.

title: West Virginia
text:
Time Served

Race and EthnicitySex


The chart below shows the average time served in prison by race and ethnicity for individuals released from prison. Black people and Hispanic people spent less time in prison than White people. Black people spent on average 2 months less in prison, and Hispanic people spent on average 1 year and 2 months less in prison compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals released from prison. The largest disparity was observed among aggravated or simple assault offenses, where Black people spent on average 8 months more in prison compared to White people.






The chart below shows the average time served in prison by sex among released individuals. Women released spent on average 1 year and 3 months less in prison compared to men.




The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and sex for individuals released from prison. The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where men spent on average 5 years and 9 months more in prison compared to women.

title: West Virginia
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Tennessee
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Tennessee, sentences under two years are released to probation automatically upon eligibility. There is a statutory staggered percentage formula (generally 30–60 percent of the maximum sentence) for parole-release eligibility, differentiated by sentencing “range” based on prior convictions. Starting in 2021, there is a growing list of 100-percent offenses, or offenses for which people are not eligible for parole.²

From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 4 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 12 percent from 2020 to 2023.

title: Tennessee
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Tennessee
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (60 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Second, in Tennesee, the percentage of the maximum sentence that corresponds to the minimum sentence depends on previous convictions. (a) “Especially Mitigated Offenders” can have lower minimum sentences at the discretion of the court. (b) “Standard Offenders” includes people with a first offense and those with a second offense, except if the previous one is Class A and the current conviction is Class A or B, with B including certain drug offenses. They are eligible for parole at 30 percent of the maximum sentence. (c) “Multiple Offenders” have between two and four prior convictions no lower than two conviction classes below the current one; they also include people with a prior Class A conviction and a current Class A or B felony. They are eligible for parole after 35 percent of the maximum sentence. (d) “Persistent Offenders” have five or more prior felony convictions no lower than the two classes below the current one or at least two Class A or a combination of three Class A or B convictions along with a current class A or B conviction. They are eligible for parole after serving 45 percent of their maximum sentence. (e) “Carreer Offenders” are sentenced for a Class A, B, or C felony and have at least prior convictions for such classes of felonies; or have three prior Class A felonies and a current Class A conviction or four prior Class A or B felony convictions and the current ones is Class A or B; or have six or more prior felony convictions and the current one is Class D or E. They are eligible for parole after serving 60 percent of the maximum sentence. When data on prior convictions was available, we imputed a minimum sentence as follows. For (a) and (b), which cannot be distinguished, 30 percent of the maximum sentence for people with no prior sentences; and people with one prior sentence, except if (i) the prior sentence was for an offense in the robbery, sex crime, “other violent”, or murder offense categories and (ii) the current current sentence is for a violent or drug offense. For (c), 35 percent of the maximum sentence for the remaining people who have up to four prior sentences for offenses that are not in the robbery, sex crime, “other violent”, murder, or drug offense categories. For (d) and (e), which cannot be distinguished, 60 percent of the maximum sentence for everyone not in the previous imputation groups. When no data on prior convictions was available, all cases were assumed to have previous sentences unless they had a shorter sentence than the previous category on record. Therefore, we updated an imputed a minimum sentence as follows. For (a) and (b), 30 percent of the maximum sentence if they had a minimum sentence of less than 35 percent of the maximum sentence on record. For (c), 35 percent of the maximum sentence for everyone else who had a minimum sentence of less than 60 percent of the maximum on record. For everyone else, we kept the previously calculated imputation.⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Tennessee. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Tennessee.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Tennessee.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Tennessee (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, June 2023), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/tennessee_doi_report_5_31_23.pdf, 6-18.² Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Tennessee (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, June 2023), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/tennessee_doi_report_5_31_23.pdf, 6-18.³ Tennessee Department of Correction, Tennessee Felon Population Update April 2024 (Nashville, TN, Decision Support: Re(Nashville, TN, Decision Support: Research and Planning, Tennessee Department of Correction, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://www.tn.gov/content/dam/tn/correction/documents/FelonApril2024.pdf, 3.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Griffith, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 6-18; TN Code § 40-35-107 (2021).⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

Measuring Disparities
Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:
(1) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group, (2) The average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility, including by offense type, and (3) The average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

title: Tennessee
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: South Carolina
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In South Carolina, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release at 25 or 33 percent of the maximum sentence with the possibility of somewhat earlier eligibility through credit earnings of 20 days per month. “No parole” crimes are not eligible for parole until they have served 85 percent of the judicial sentence, with three days of credit per month.²

title: South Carolina
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 45 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 18 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 37 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 4 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 5 percent from 2020 to 2023.

title: South Carolina
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: South Carolina
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the percentage of the maximum sentence (25 percent for those who’s most serious offense was non-violent, and 33 for those whose most serious offense was violent, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Second, in South Carolina, people with two or more violent offenses are not eligible for parole. Thus, when the data allowed seeing previous sentences, people with two or more sentences serving a term for a violent offense and with a previous sentence for a violent offense were not imputed a minimum sentence. When the data did not allow seeing previous terms, no minimum sentence was imputed for people serving a term for a violent offense and who did not already have a PEY on record. ⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for South Carolina. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for South Carolina.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for South Carolina.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: Pennsylvania
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Pennsylvania judges have discretion to set the durations of minimum terms to parole-release eligibility, which can be any length up to 50 percent of the maximum. However, there is a minimum of 10 years for those convicted of a second violent crime and 25 years for those who committed a violent crime after two or more convictions for violent crimes.²

title: Pennsylvania
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Pennsylvania
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (50 percent). Second, the absolute minimum sentence of when the most serious offense was non-violent and 25 years for people with offenses of more than . years when the most serious offense was violent was computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute minimum sentence if the latter was shorter. Third, in Pennsylvania, there is a minimum of (a) 10 years for those convicted of a second violent crime and (b) 25 years for those who committed a violent crime after two or more convictions for violent crimes. Therefore, when the data allowed seeing previous terms, we imputed a minimum sentence of (a) 10 years for people serving a sentence of at least 10 years for a violent crime if they had one previous sentence, it was for a violent crime, and they did not have a shorter minimum sentence on record; and of (b) 25 years for people with two previous sentences who were serving a sentence for a violent crime of at least 25 years, had a previous sentence for a violent crime, and did not have a shorter minimum on record. When the data did not allow seeing previous sentences, we conservatively assumed everyone could have a prior violent offense and (a) imputed a minimum of 10 years for people serving a maximum sentence of at least 10 years for a violent crime and did not have a shorter minimum sentence on record, and then (b) imputed a minimum of 25 years for people serving a maximum sentence of at least 25 years for a violent crime and did not have a shorter minimum sentence on record. This last step could overwrite cases in (a). ⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Pennsylvania. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Pennsylvania.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Pennsylvania.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: Pennsylvania
text:
Measuring Disparities
Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:
(1) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group, (2) The average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility, including by offense type, and (3) The average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

Black people spent more time in prison after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Hispanic people spent less time in prison after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Black people spent on average 5 months more past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 4 months less past parole eligibility compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among aggravated or simple assault offenses, where Black people spent on average 1 year and 4 months more in prison compared to White people.






The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by sex for individuals still incarcerated. Women who were still incarcerated spent on average 2 years and 3 months less past parole eligibility compared to men.

title: Pennsylvania
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: North Dakota
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In North Dakota, most people who are incarcerated are eligible for discretionary parole release soon after they enter prison. The parole board has broad discretion to set minimum terms to parole release consideration for each prisoner individually. Violent offenses are subject to an 85-percent eligibility rule.²

title: North Dakota
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 70 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 9 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 21 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 5 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility increased by 4 percent from 2020 to 2023.

We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for North Dakota.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for North Dakota.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. From 2010 to 2020, the number of people released from prison increased 30 percent, from 1,009 in 2010 to 1,312 in 2020.





In the most recent year of data available, 61 percent of parole-eligible people released in North Dakota were released past their parole eligibility year, which is a decrease of 18 percent from 2010.

title: North Dakota
text:
Conditional Releases
Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. 95 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

60 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.

46 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.

title: North Dakota
text:
Offense Types
25 percent of people released from prison were in prison for violent offenses and 75 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people released from prison were drug offenses (41 percent).

title: North Dakota
text:
Measuring Disparities
Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:
(1) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group, (2) The average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility, including by offense type, and (3) The average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

Black, non-Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past their parole eligibility at the same rate as White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in North Dakota.

Hispanic people were 20 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility compared to White people, when accounting for population sizes in North Dakota.Analysis of disparities for Hispanic people should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.

Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility

Race and EthnicitySex


The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. Black people and Hispanic people spent less time in prison after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Black people spent on average 1 month less past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 2 months less past parole eligibility compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where Hispanic people spent on average 3 years more in prison compared to White people.






The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by sex for individuals still incarcerated. Women who were still incarcerated spent on average 7 months less past parole eligibility compared to men.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and sex for individuals still incarcerated. The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals released from prison. The largest disparity was observed among public order offenses, where Hispanic people spent on average 9 months more in prison compared to White people.






The chart below shows the average time served in prison by sex among released individuals. Women released spent on average 7 months less in prison compared to men.

title: North Dakota
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: New Hampshire
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Most prison sentences in New Hampshire include judicial minimum and maximum terms. A judicial minimum term may not exceed one-half of the maximum term.²

title: New Hampshire
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 38 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 21 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 42 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2014 to 2019, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 2 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility remained around 37 percent from 2019 to 2023. Note: 2020 data uses projections.

title: New Hampshire
text:
Sentence Lengths
