title: New Hampshire
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: New Hampshire
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (50 percent). Second, in New Hampshire, a disciplinary period of 150 days is added to any minimum sentence of more than one year, which can be reduced by credits. Thus, we added half a year to the minimum sentence of anyone with a maximum sentence of two or more years. ⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ Some years had to be excluded for this reason for New Hampshire. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for New Hampshire.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for New Hampshire.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: New Hampshire
text:
Endnotes
¹ New Hampshire Parole Board, “Home,” accessed November 12, 2024, https://www.paroleboard.nh.gov.² Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: New Hampshire (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, February 2023), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-02/new_hampshire_doi_report_2_13_22.pdf, 9.³ Tom Jarvis and Scott Merrill, “NBHA Prison Series: Maintaining Order and Dispensing Punishment,” New Hampshire Bar Association, accessed October 1, 2024, https://www.nhbar.org/nhba-prison-series-maintaining-order-and-dispensing-punishment/.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Griffith, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 9.⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: Montana
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
For most people who are incarcerated in Montana, first eligibility for discretionary parole release occurs at the 25-percent mark of their judicial maximum sentences. Sentencing courts in Montana have the unusual power to order in individual cases that people who are incarcerated will never enjoy eligibility for discretionary release or that their minimum terms to release eligibility will be delayed past the 25-percent mark of their maximum sentences. Montana is one of a few states that have abolished good time, earned time, and related credits for people in prison to reduce their judicial maximum sentences or the period of time to first parole-release eligibility.²

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. title: Montana
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the percentage of the maximum sentence (25 percent). All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Montana. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Montana.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Montana.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total custody population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the custody population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: Montana
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Mississippi
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Mississippi, for people in prison convicted of nonviolent crimes, parole-release eligibility generally occurs at the 25-percent mark of their judicial maximum terms or at 10 years, whichever is less. For most people in prison convicted of violent offenses, parole-release eligibility comes at the 50-percent milestone of their maximum terms or at 20 years, whichever is less.²

title: Mississippi

In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 34 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 25 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 41 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 29 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 25 percent from 2020 to 2023.

title: Mississippi
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Mississippi
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the percentage of the maximum sentence (25 percent for those who’s most serious offense was non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense was violent). Second, the absolute minimum sentence of 10 years when the most serious offense was non-violent and 20 years for people with offenses of more than . years when the most serious offense was violent was computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute minimum sentence if the latter was shorter. Third, in Mississippi people convicted three times are generally ineligible for parole. Therefore, when the data allowed identifying previous offenses, no PEY was imputed for people with three or more offenses unless they already had a PEY on record. When the data did not allow seeing previous sentences, no PEY was imputed for people who did not already have one on record. ⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Mississippi. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Mississippi.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Mississippi.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total custody population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the custody population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: Mississippi
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Massachusetts
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Massachusetts, sentencing courts decide the length of minimum terms, which can range from 0 to 100 percent of the maximum sentence. Credit-based deductions from both minimum and maximum terms are statutorily capped at 35 percent.²

title: Massachusetts
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 35 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 14 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 50 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 180 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 17 percent from 2020 to 2023.

title: Massachusetts
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Massachusetts
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (. percent). All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Massachusetts. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Massachusetts.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Massachusetts.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total custody population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the custody population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: Massachusetts
text:
Endnotes
¹ Alexis Lee Watts, Edward E. Rhine, and Catherine McDonough, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – Massachusetts (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2018), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/massachusetts_parole_profile.pdf, 13.² Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Massachusetts (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, July 2022), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-07/massachusetts_doi_report_7_7_22.pdf, 6-7.³ Massachusetts Department of Correction, Prison Population Trends Report – Calendar Year 2023 (Milford, MA: Research and Planning Divi(Milford, MA: Research and Planning Division, Massachusetts Department of Correction, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://www.mass.gov/doc/prison-population-trends-2023/download, 9 – 11.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Lukac, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 6-7.⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: Massachusetts
text:
Measuring Disparities
Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:
(1) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group, (2) The average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility, including by offense type, and (3) The average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

title: Massachusetts
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Louisiana
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Louisiana eliminated discretionary parole in 2024, but it is included among the paroling states in this project because the majority of the population remains eligible for parole release under the previous system. Prior to 2024, there were six tiers of eligibility formulas in Louisiana for different sentence classes: (1) People convicted of a nonviolent felony and with no prior convictions are eligible at 25 percent of their maximum term; (2) people with a second nonviolent felony or who have a first violent non-sex offense but are not a “habitual offender” at one-third; (3) people convicted for the first time of statutorily designated “crimes of violence,” excluding sex offenses, at 65 percent; (4) people convicted of a second felony offense at two-thirds; (5) people convicted of a first or second sex offense or a second “crime of violence” at 75 percent; and (6) people convicted of a third or more felony offense are not eligible for parole.²

title: Louisiana
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Louisiana
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the percentage of the maximum sentence (25 percent for those who’s most serious offense was non-violent, and 65 for those whose most serious offense was violent). Second, Third, in Louisiana, there are four more tiers of eligibility than in most states for people with previous convictions. (a) People with a second nonviolent felony or who have a first violent non-sex offense not considered a “crime of violence” and who are not a “habitual offender” are eligible at one third of their maximum sentence. (b) People convicted of a second felony offense are eligible at two thirds. (c) People convicted of a first or second sex offense or a second “crime of violence” are eligible at 75 percent. (d) People convicted of a third or more felony offense are not eligible for parole. While only some violent crimes are “crimes of violence”, all violent offense categories in the dataset included at least one “crimes of violence;” thus, anyone with such an offense category was conservatively assumed to potentially have been sentenced for a “crime of violence” and have higher parole restrictions. Therefore, when the dataset allowed identifying previous sentences, we adjusted the previously calculated imputation. We imputed (a) a minimum sentence of one third of the maximum sentence (a.1) for people with two sentences for non-violent offenses and (a.2) for people with one violent offense who did not fit group (c.2) below. We also imputed (b) a minimum of two thirds for people with two sentences with the current one being for a violent offense but the first one not; and (c) a minimum of 75 percent (c.1) for people with one or two sentences in the rape and sexual assault category and (c.2) for people with two sentences for violent crimes who did not have a shorter minimum on record. (d) We did not impute a minimum sentence for people with three sentences. Because the NCRP data did not allow distinguishing “crimes of violence” from other violent offenses, everyone sentenced for a first violent crime was imputed the abovementioned minimum of 65 percent of the maximum sentence instead of the minimum of one third that corresponds to those in situation (a) unless they had a shorter PEY on record, in which case they kept the lower imputation. When the dataset did not allow identifying previous sentences, we adjusted the imputation more conservatively, assuming that everyone could have three offenses and not be eligible for parole if they did not already have a PEY on record. For those who had a PEY on record, we updated the previously calculated imputation with (a) a minimum sentence of one third of the maximum sentence for people serving a term for a non-violent offense who did not have a shorter minimum on record, and (b) a minimum sentence of two thirds for people serving a term for a non-sex violent offense without a shorter minimum on record and who were not in the next group, and (c) a minimum sentence of 75 percent for everyone else serving a sentence for a violent offense without a shorter minimum on record.⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Louisiana. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Louisiana.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Louisiana.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: Louisiana
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Kentucky
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Kentucky, the parole board sets parole eligibility for many offenses by policy: A maximum sentence of 40 years or more is eligible in eight years; maximum terms of 2–39 years are eligible at 20 percent of the maximum sentence; and maximum terms of one year and up to but not including two years are eligible at four months. By statute, nonviolent Class D offenses are eligible after serving 15 percent of the maximum. Also by statute, some violent offenses are eligible at 85 percent. People sentenced as “persistent felony offenders” for class A, B, or C felonies have a minimum sentence of 10 years and are not parole eligible if they are “violent” and sentenced as first degree “persistent felony offenders.”²

title: Kentucky
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Kentucky
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (20 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Second, the absolute minimum sentence of 3.96 months when the most serious offense was non-violent and 8 years for people with offenses of more than 39 years when the most serious offense was violent was computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute minimum sentence if the latter was longer. Third, Third, in Kentucky, (a) people convicted of a certian classes of felonies have a minimum and maximum sentence of at least ten years if they have been convicted of two prior felonies, and (b) are not eligible if the current conviction is for a violent offense in the first degree. Therefore, (a) since every offense category includes the types with a minimum sentence of at least 20 years, everyone was imputed a minimum of ten years if they did not have a shorter minimum or maximum on record or a larger minimum imputed. If the dataset allowed identifying previous offenses, this imputation was applied only to those with two or more prior sentences. (b) If the person’s latest sentence was for a violent offense, and the person had two or more prior sentences or no data was available about prior sentences, no minimum sentence was imputed. ⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Kentucky. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Kentucky.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Kentucky.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.
