title: Connecticut
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In general, people in Connecticut serving a definite sentence of more than two years where the underlying offense does not involve the use or threat of physical force become eligible for parole after serving one-half of the maximum sentence length. The parole board determines the applicability of the use of force criterion, which requires a person to serve 85 percent of the maximum to be eligible for parole release.²

title: Connecticut
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 35 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 33 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 32 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 24 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 21 percent from 2020 to 2023.

title: Connecticut
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Connecticut
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense was non-violent, and 85 for those whose most serious offense was violent). All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Connecticut. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Connecticut.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Connecticut.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

text:
Endnotes
¹ Alexis Lee Watts, Catherine A. McDonough, and Cecelia Klingele, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – Connecticut (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2017), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/700687_new_connecticut_parole_profile3.pdf, 12.² Kevin R. Reitz, Matthew Jacobs, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Connecticut (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, February 2021), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/connecticut_doi_report_2_9_21.pdf, 5-7.³ Connecticut Department of Correction Research Unit, Average Confined Inmate Population and Legal Status (Wethersfield, CT: Connecticut Department of Correct(Wethersfield, CT: Connecticut Department of Correction, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://portal.ct.gov/-/media/doc/pdf/monthlystat/stat01012024.pdf, 1.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Jacobs, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 5-7.⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: Connecticut
text:
Measuring Disparities
Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:
(1) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group, (2) The average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility, including by offense type, and (3) The average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility

Race and EthnicitySex


The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. Black people spent more time in prison after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Hispanic people spent less time in prison after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Black people spent on average 1 month more past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 7 months less past parole eligibility compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among drug offenses, where Black people spent on average 1 year and 8 months more in prison compared to White people.






The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by sex for individuals still incarcerated. Women who were still incarcerated spent on average 1 year and 3 months less past parole eligibility compared to men.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and sex for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where men spent on average 9 years and 5 months more in prison compared to women.

title: Connecticut
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Hawaii
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Hawaii, for most felonies, the sentencing court must impose a maximum sentence according to the offense grade (5, 10, 20, or life), with no discretion to determine the minimum. Minimum terms are set by the parole board, except when a mandatory minimum statute applies.²

If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.

Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Hawaii (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, May 2023), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-05/hawaii_doi_report_05_17_23_0.pdf, 6-7.³ Department of Public Safety, Department of Public Safety End of Month Population Report – December 31, 2023 (Honolulu: HI, Hawaii Departm(Honolulu: HI, Hawaii Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation, Hawaii Department of Public Safety, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://dcr.hawaii.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Pop-Reports-EOM-2023-12-31.pdf.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Lukac, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 6-7.⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:

title: Hawaii
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Iowa
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Generally, prison sentences in Iowa include a judicial maximum term but no minimum term. Most people who are incarcerated are eligible for discretionary parole release immediately upon admission.²

title: Iowa
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 72 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 3 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 25 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 8 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility increased by 2 percent from 2020 to 2023.

title: Iowa
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Iowa
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the percentage of the maximum sentence (0 percent). Second, in Iowa, while most people are eligible upon admission, there are three situations that have minimum sentences. (a) A minimum of five years is applied for people who displayed, possessed, or represented he was in possession of a deadly weapon in the course of a forcible felony. Thus, we imputed that minimum sentence to everyone admitted for a violent offense unless they had a shorter minimum or maximum sentence on record. (b) A minimum sentence of three years is applied to people convicted of a class C or D felony and who have two prior felonies. Consequently, when a dataset indicating previous terms was available, anyone with two or more prior sentences was imputed a minimum of three years unless they had a shorter minimum or maximum sentence on record. When no data on previous terms was available, everyone with a minimum sentence of three years or more or no minimum was imputed a minimum of three years, unless they had a shorter minimum or maximum sentence on record.(c) Those who conspire to manufacture or deliver anphetamine or metaphetamine to a minor have a minimum sentence of ten years. Therefore, we imputed a minimum sentence of ten years for people with drug offenses and a maximum sentence of at least ten years if they did not have a shorter minimum sentence on record. ⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ Some years had to be excluded for this reason for Iowa. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. Some years previously excluded were then included for this reason for Iowa.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Iowa.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: Maryland
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Maryland, people who are convicted of nonviolent crimes with sentences of more than six months become eligible for parole after serving one-fourth of the maximum sentence. People in prison convicted of a violent crime become eligible for parole after serving one-half of the maximum.²

title: Maryland
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 30 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 21 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 50 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 22 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility increased by 1 percent from 2020 to 2023.

57 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 43 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense types among people in prison past parole eligibility were robbery offenses (16 percent) and property offenses (16 percent).

title: Maryland
text:
Sentence Lengths
36 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.

title: Maryland
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Maryland
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the percentage of the maximum sentence (25 percent for those who’s most serious offense was non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense was violent). All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Maryland. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Maryland.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Maryland.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

Rhine, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – Maryland (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2018), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/maryland_parole_profile.pdf, 11.² Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Maryland (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, December 2020), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/maryland_doi_report_3_3_21.pdf, 6-8.³ “Maryland Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services Division of Correction FY 2023 Population Overview,” Maryland Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services, accessed October 1, 2024, https://www.dpscs.state.md.us/community_releases/DOC-Annual-Data-Dashboard.shtml.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Lukac, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 6-8.⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: Maryland
text:
Measuring Disparities
Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:
(1) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group, (2) The average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility, including by offense type, and (3) The average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

text:
Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility

Race and EthnicitySex


The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. Black people spent more time in prison after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Hispanic people spent less time in prison after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Black people spent on average 1 month more past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 9 months less past parole eligibility compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among negligent manslaughter offenses, where Black people spent on average 6 months more in prison compared to White people.






The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by sex for individuals still incarcerated. Women who were still incarcerated spent on average 4 months less past parole eligibility compared to men.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and sex for individuals still incarcerated. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where men spent on average 1 year and 10 months more in prison compared to women.

The chart below shows the average time served in prison by race and ethnicity for individuals released from prison. Black people and Hispanic people spent more time in prison than White people. Black people spent on average 2 months more in prison, and Hispanic people spent on average 4 months more in prison compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and race and ethnicity for individuals released from prison. The largest disparity was observed among drug offenses, where Hispanic people spent on average 1 year and 6 months more in prison compared to White people.






The chart below shows the average time served in prison by sex among released individuals. Women released spent on average 1 year and 3 months less in prison compared to men.




The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and sex for individuals released from prison. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where men spent on average 3 years and 6 months more in prison compared to women.

title: Maryland
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Michigan
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Michigan, by statute, fixed maximum terms must be included in most judicial sentences. Minimum terms can be as short as the judge elects but may not exceed two-thirds of the maximum sentence. There is no system of good time, earned time, or similar credits that may be accumulated by people who are incarcerated as a discount.²

text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Michigan
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (67 percent). All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ Some years had to be excluded for this reason for Michigan. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. Some years had to be excluded for this reason for Michigan. Consequently, data reported for Michigan is from 2017, except for the 2023 estimate and the trends data.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2017, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Michigan.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: Michigan
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Missouri
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Missouri, minimum terms to parole-release eligibility for most people who are incarcerated are established by regulations by the parole board, ranging from 15 percent of the maximum sentence for nonviolent offenses to 33 percent for violent offenses, sex offenses, and child abuse. By statute, dangerous offenses serve 85 percent of the maximum to be eligible, and repeated commitments to prison for other serious offenses result in increasing minimums of 40, 50, and 70 percent.²

title: Missouri
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 50 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 23 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 26 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 6 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 7 percent from 2020 to 2023.
