title: Missouri
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Missouri
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (25 percent for those who’s most serious offense was non-violent, and 33 for those whose most serious offense was violent, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Missouri. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Missouri.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Missouri.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: Missouri
text:
Endnotes
¹ Alexis Lee Watts, Kevin R. Reitz, and Julie Matucheski, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – Missouri (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2018), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/missouri_parole_profile.pdf, 12.² Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Missouri (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, July 2022), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-07/missouri_doi_report_7_27_22.pdf, 6-8.³ Missouri Department of Corrections, Profile of the Institutional and Supervised Offender Population – June 30, 2023 (Columbia, MO: Missouri Department o(Columbia, MO: Missouri Department of Corrections, 2023), accessed October 1, 2024, https://doc.mo.gov/media/pdf/offender-profile-fy23, 1.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Lukac, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 6-8.⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

title: Missouri
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Nevada
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Nevada, for each of five felony grades, the court sets a minimum term that can be no less than one year and no more than 40 percent of the judicial maximum sentence. Nevada has a multilayered system of good time and earned time credits, which are deducted from the minimum and maximum terms. The advancement of parole-release eligibility is limited to 58 percent of the minimum term.²

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.

Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Nevada
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (40 percent). Second, the absolute minimum sentence of 1 year was computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute minimum sentence if the latter was. All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Nevada. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Nevada.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Nevada.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total custody population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the custody population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: Nevada
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: New York
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In New York, most people who are incarcerated for violent, sex, and drug felony offenses are not eligible for discretionary parole, although their sentence can be reduced roughly 14 percent by “good time” credits. All other people serving a sentence for an offense that carries a judicial maximum of at least three years but not life are eligible for discretionary parole after a minimum set by the court in a range between one year to one-third of the maximum sentence. The minimum can also be reduced by “good time” credits.²

Most recent data shows that 14 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 5 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 4 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 50 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 16 percent from 2020 to 2023.

title: New York
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: New York
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the percentage of the maximum sentence (33 percent for those who’s most serious offense was non-violent, and . for those whose most serious offense was violent). Second, the absolute minimum sentence of 1 year when the most serious offense was non-violent and for people with offenses of more than . years when the most serious offense was violent was computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute minimum sentence if the latter was longer. Third, in New York, parole only exists for non-violent offenses with statutorily maximum sentences of at least 3 years. Thus, we assumed that everyone with a sentence of less than 3 years and who does not already have PEY is not parole eligible. Fourth, most drug offenses are not eligible for parole, so no PEY was imputed for people with those offenses. However, even before our estimate, the raw NCRP data for New York indicated an inflated number of people past PEY compared to a New York report that, on Jan. 1 2018, 60 percent of people were not parole eligible. Additional analyses suggests that New York submitted not the discretionary PEY but the time at which people could be released on good time allowance or merit time allowance. Therefore, for New York, we only used the imputation and discarded the reported and the one obtained from matching to previous years’ reports, and only used the imputation. ⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for New York. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for New York.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for New York.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Oklahoma
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
The Oklahoma parole board has authority to grant discretionary parole release only to prisoners convicted of nonviolent offenses. For prisoners convicted of violent offenses, the parole board is limited to making recommendations to release to the governor. For offenses committed between July 1, 1998, and November 1, 2018, people who are incarcerated become eligible after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences. For offenses committed on or after November 1, 2018, people become eligible after one-fourth of their judicial maximum terms. People convicted of violent offenses become eligible after 85 percent of their maximum.²

From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 28 percent. Our projection estimates that the percent of people past their initial parole eligibility decreased by 27 percent from 2020 to 2023.

title: Oklahoma
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: Oklahoma
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the percentage of the maximum sentence (25 percent). All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ No year had to be excluded for this reason for Oklahoma. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for Oklahoma.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for Oklahoma.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

Endnotes
¹ Alexis Lee Watts, Brendan Delaney, and Edward E. Rhine, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – Oklahoma (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2018), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/oklahoma_parole_profile.pdf, 11.² Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, Bree Crye, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Oklahoma (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, June 2022), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-06/oklahoma_doi_report_6_24_22.pdf, 8-12.³ Oklahoma Department of Correction, Daily Statewide Population Report – 01/01/2024 (Oklahoma City, OK: Oklahoma Depart(Oklahoma City, OK: Oklahoma Department of Correction, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://oklahoma.gov/content/dam/ok/en/doc/documents/population/count-sheet/2024/ICON%20COUNT%2001.01.2024.pdf, 1.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Crye, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 8-12.⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

text:
Measuring Disparities
Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:
(1) Relative Rate Indexes (RRIs), which measure the rate at which one group experiences an outcome relative to another group, (2) The average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility, including by offense type, and (3) The average time individuals served in prison, also broken down by offense.

title: Oklahoma
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Rhode Island
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations
Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: South Dakota
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In South Dakota, by statute there is a continuum of staggered parole-release formulas from the 25- to 75-percent mark based on offense severity and criminal history. For crimes committed after July 1, 2023, sentences for designated serious crimes have been made nonparolable. A large percentage of people who are incarcerated are assigned “initial parole dates” shortly after admission, with the expectation that they will be released without a hearing before the parole board if they comply with the requirements of their “individual program directives.”²

title: South Dakota
text:
Key Terms and Data Sources

Key Terms

Parole Eligibility Year (PEY): The first year in which a person is eligible for discretionary parole, determined by state-specific sentencing practices. PEY reflects the point at which a person who is incarcerated can be considered for release, though it does not guarantee release. If a parole board has discretion to set that date, PEY is the first year that the board can set a hearing.
Parole-Eligible Individual: A person who is serving a sentence for a criminal offense who remains incarcerated past their PEY. This excludes individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year.
Projections: Statistical models used to estimate future values based on historical trends. Here, projections estimate the number of people incarcerated past their parole eligibility date in 2023, based on trends from 2010 to 2020.
Racial Disparities: Racial disparities refer to measurable differences in outcomes, access, or treatment between racial or ethnic groups. Racial disparities have many causes and are manifested in statistics like arrest rates, sentencing lengths, and prison populations. Racial disparities should not be confused with individual acts of racism or prejudice. While individual bias can contribute to disparities, these patterns exist at a systemic level across institutions.
Relative Rate Index (RRI): A comparative measure that evaluates the rate of an outcome (such as incarceration or remaining in prison past parole eligibility) between two groups. For this project, RRI compares the rates for Black and Hispanic populations to those of the White population.



Data Sources

Parole Eligibility: For parole-related metrics, including the year that an individual becomes eligible for parole, data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) Selected Variables was the primary source. NCRP collects individual-level data on prison admissions, releases, custody populations, and parole status changes. The data includes demographics, offenses, sentences, admission/release types, and time served from individual records, with collection ongoing since 1983. At least 47 states contributed data during the time period of our study, but this data varied in quality and, as a result, not all states are included in our analyses. While the most recent NCRP data extends only to 2020, projections for 2023 were made to provide up-to-date estimates.
Prison Populations, prior to 2023: The number of people who are incarcerated by race, ethnicity, and sex was derived from the Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoners Series and the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS). These datasets are collected annually from all 50 states and provide detailed aggregate information on prison populations for each state, including data on race and sex of people who are incarcerated. The most recent data available at the time of our analysis was for 2022.
Prison Populations, 2023: Our modelling approach required more recent prison population numbers than were available from the NPS, so we manually collected prison population data from state departments of corrections for 2023.3

title: South Dakota
text:
Estimation Methodology
We began by limiting our population to individuals incarcerated in prison, excluding people who were returned to prison from parole and those serving a life sentence or a sentence of less than one year. Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier were calculated in three steps. (1) We started with the PEY variable in the NCRP for the state and year of interest. (2) Next, we looked at the PEY provided in previous years’ reports to identify the earliest PEY recorded for a person. This can help capture PEY that is missing for people who should have one and likely had their PEY deleted or replaced with a new date after a failed hearing. (3) When PEY was missing, we used the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY. We also replaced the reported PEY if our rules-based estimate was earlier than the PEY reported in the NCRP data. Because the estimate is conservative, the results may undercount the number of people in prison past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous years’ reports without an NCRP identifier available across reports, we create our own identifier based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general) terms count, sentences count, most serious prior sentence, last serious prior offense, and second to last serious prior offense. If in any year multiple people had the same identifier, the latest PEY among them was selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year of interest, that one was selected. The process was repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code. It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, each person, was assigns a minimum sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the maximum sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense was non-violent, and 75 for those whose most serious offense was violent). Second, in South Dakota, the percentage of time required for parole depends on prior convictions and the offense type. There is a complex classification of offenses in classes and a relatively small difference in percentages between most classes of violent and classes of nonviolent crimes. Thus, when data on previous sentences was available, everyone sentenced for a nonviolent offense was conservatively imputed a minimum sentence of 35 percent of the maximum if they were serving their first sentence, 40 percent for those serving a second sentence, and 50 percent for those serving a third sentence or more. For people with violent offenses, a minimum sentence of 50 percent of the maximum sentence was imputed for those serving a first sentence, 65 percent for those serving a second sentence, and 75 percent for those serving a third sentence or more. These imputations replaced the more conservative one previously calculated. For those serving a murder sentence, no minimum was imputed. While people convicted for Class A and B felonies are eligible for parole at 85 percent to 100 percent of their maximum sentence, no adjustment was made for them because they have life sentences and were excluded from the analysis. When data about previous sentences was not available, everyone was assumed to have two prior sentences, so the previously calculated more conservative imputation was maintained. ⁴ All fractions were rounded up. If the rules-based imputation was earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it was used as the best estimate.Results were then checked against available data from the states about people past parole eligibility and parole hearing approval rates. We also checked whether estimates and the percent missing PEY were consistent with the state’s eligibility rules and for the potential influence of any missing data in the imputation.Data was excluded for any year when more than 33 percent of the cases were missing the admission year or the maximum sentence length variables, as well as for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases were missing PEY after imputation. .⁵ Some years had to be excluded for this reason for South Dakota. Even if missing data was under this threshold, if the missingness appeared to follow a pattern that might bias the estimate, that year’s data was excluded for the state. This was evident, for instance, when the percent of people past PEY substantially dropped as a state stopped recording a variable for newly admitted people. If due to missingness there is reason to believe there is likely an undercount of people past PEY of a few percentage points, but it is not certainly a major undercount, this is noted at the top. This is the case, for instance, when the state’s rules make rules-based imputation too conservative for some people and many people were still missing a PEY after looking at previous records. Conversely, if a state was missing more than the abovementioned thresholds any year but due to other available data that missingness was not problematic, the year was not excluded. No year had to be excluded for this reason for South Dakota.Because there was a change in the trend of percentage of people past PEY in 2020 in many states, likely due to the COVID-19 pandemic, figures that report data from one year are from 2019, the latest year with reliable estimates available for South Dakota.Our models were restricted to a subgroup of the prison population, excluding all individuals who were returned to prison from parole and people with life sentences or sentences of less than one year. Estimates for the percentage of people who had passed their PEY in 2023 were derived from a regression model based on data from 2010 to 2020. This regression accounted for state-specific trends in the annual changes to the percentage of people past their PEY over the decade, as well as the impact of special COVID-19 policies in 2020. (Technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both factors was used.) The total number of people past their PEY in 2023 was calculated by multiplying the 2023 restricted subgroup of the population by the estimated percentage of people past their PEY.The size of the restricted subgroup of the population in 2023 was estimated using a regression model based on the percentage of the total jurisdiction population represented by this subgroup between 2010 and 2020. For data from 2010 to 2022, the jurisdiction population reported in the National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) was used.

title: South Dakota
text:
Endnotes
¹ Alexis Lee Watts, Brendan Delaney, and Kevin R. Reitz, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – South Dakota (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2019), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/south_carolina_parole_profile.pdf, 11.² Kevin R. Reitz, Bree Crye, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: South Dakota (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, August 2023), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/south_dakota_doi_report_8_10_23.pdf, 1, 7-9.³ “Adult Corrections 11/30/2023,” South Dakota Department of Corrections, accessed October 1, 2024, https://web.archive.org/web/20240101091645/https:/doc.sd.gov/documents/AdultPopulationNovember30203.pdf.⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43; Reitz, Crye, Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion, 7-9; 22 SDC §§ 16-12, 16-15, 19-1, 30-7, 22-1(6).⁵ NCRP provides three datasets for each year: “year-end population,” “releases,” and “terms.” The latter includes both each year’s year-end population and releases, along with data on previous incarceration terms for each person. In addition to having more data, it is not always completely consistent with the data in the other two datasets, with some cases or variables missing some years. Because of this, the analysis on the year-end population and the releases population was replicated with the “terms” dataset. When the “terms” dataset identified a higher proportion of the relevant population as being past their PEY in a given year and state—that is, the “terms” estimate was less conservative—that dataset was selected—except in rare cases where that higher proportion was an artifact of missing data in the “terms” dataset.

Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Incarceration Past Parole Eligibility: This metric quantifies the relative likelihood of remaining incarcerated past parole eligibility, comparing Black and Hispanic individuals to White individuals and men to women. An RRI above 1.0 signals a higher likelihood of delayed parole release, whereas an RRI below 1.0 indicates a lower likelihood. These disparities shed light on inequities in the parole release process.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.

This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. title: Texas
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Texas, people with lower-severity offenses become eligible for parole when their time served plus good time credits equals one-quarter of the maximum sentence or 15 years, whichever is less. People with higher-severity offenses become eligible for parole at one-half of the maximum sentence or 30 years, whichever is less, but never less than two years and without reduction by good time.²
